there is a real number $ x $ greater than 1 that is not an integer and satisfies the inequality:

$$
\{x\}+\left\{x^{3}\right\}+\left\{x^{5}\right\}+\cdots+\left\{x^{99}\right\}<\frac{1}{2^{99}}
$$

the fractional part of a number is defined as the difference between the number and its integer part, and it always lies in the interval $[0, 1)$. for example, if $ x = 2.5 $, then $ \{x\} = 0.5 $, and if $ x = 3.7 $, then $ \{x\} = 0.7 $. this fractional part is crucial in this problem because it allows us to analyze the behavior of the sum of fractional parts of powers of $ x $.

the inequality involves the sum of fractional parts of $ x $, $ x^3 $, $ x^5 $, and so on, up to $ x^{99} $. the left-hand side of the inequality is a sum of 50 terms, each of which is the fractional part of a power of $ x $, and the right-hand side is a very small number, $ \frac{1}{2^{99}} $, which is approximately $ 1.17 \times 10^{-30} $.
